nativeRange.habitat: The Blue Gudgeon Dartfish inhabits coastal lagoons, bays, reef passes, and outer reef slopes over hard substrate mixed with rubble and sand patches. It is most commonly found between 5 and 15 metres depth, though it has been recorded as deep as 80 metres. Adults dig or occupy burrows in the sand, retreating into them when threatened, and feed on small drifting animals (zooplankton) carried by the current just a few metres above the bottom.

decorPreferences: open sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing
live rock with crevices for shelter
open swimming space above the substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on current-borne zooplankton hovering up to about 3 m above its burrow. In captivity generally accepts mysis shrimp, brine shrimp (vitamin-enriched), copepods, finely chopped krill, and frozen carnivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day (commonly 3 or more) are generally advised, particularly during acclimation when individuals tend to be shy.

summary: The Blue Gudgeon Dartfish is a peaceful, reef-safe Indo-Pacific species generally considered beginner-friendly. It tends to hover just above the sand bed near its burrow and is widely regarded as a strong jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is essential.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in an established reef or fish-only-with-live-rock aquarium with a sandy substrate (commonly suggested 5+ cm deep), loose coral rubble for shelter, and open swimming space above the sand bed. This species has been reported to excavate or occupy burrows and tends to hover just above them while feeding on suspended plankton. It tends to be shy during the first days after introduction; multiple small feedings have been reported to help draw it out and encourage feeding responses.

Widely regarded as a jumper — a tight-fitting lid with no gaps is generally considered essential. Escape risk is high, consistent with most dartfish in the hobby.

Generally considered peaceful toward all tank mates, including conspecifics, and has been reported to do well kept singly, as a mated pair, or in small groups of 2–4 individuals introduced simultaneously (larger groups tend to be less seclusive). Best avoided with overtly aggressive or predatory tankmates.

Note: this species is distinct from the closely related Lyretail Blue Gudgeon Dartfish (Ptereleotris hanae), which is also sold under the same common name. The two are distinct species and should not be confused.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been well documented in commonly available hobbyist literature. In the wild, adults have been reported to live in monogamous pairs sharing a burrow, but specific aquarium spawning protocols are not established.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding protocols are not established in commonly available hobbyist sources. In the wild, pairs share burrows and are presumably substrate/burrow-spawners, but specific aquarium breeding has not been well documented.
